PRJ_05 · 系统影响 · 2025

Coffee Grinder
RevEng

A full reverse engineering teardown and ecological redesign of the Vencier CM168 electric coffee grinder — identifying manufacturing processes, quantifying the carbon footprint, and proposing a 16% CO₂ reduction through material substitution and Design for Disassembly.

Context

Year 1 Materials & Manufacturing
Commissioned by Lisa's Eco-Innovations

Role

Sole Analyst
Teardown · Eco-Audit · Redesign

Tech Stack

ANSYS Granta EduPack Ashby Plot DfD / BOM

Outcome

33-Part BOM · −16.3% CO₂
rPET Redesign Proposal

01 · The Challenge

Cheap, capable,
but ecologically
blind.

The Vencier CM168 electric coffee grinder (£14.99 on Amazon) represents the dominant model in consumer-grade small appliances: a 150W AC motor, 70g grinding capacity, one-button pressure-sensitive operation, and a transparent PMMA lid. Structurally competent. Ecologically opaque.

The brief from Lisa's Eco-Innovations (LEI): conduct a full disassembly, reverse-engineer the materials and manufacturing processes, quantify the environmental footprint using Eco-Audit, and propose a redesign that reduces carbon intensity without changing the motor architecture.

Research shows that 80% of a product's environmental impact is determined at the design stage. This project treats the teardown not as destruction — but as diagnosis.

Vencier CM168 · product specification

Motor150W AC

Blade speed~20,000 RPM

Total carbon footprint5.41 kg CO₂

Components identified

02 · The Teardown · Bill of Materials

Open it up.
Read what's
inside.

The two-stage teardown — macro disassembly into four subcomponents (lid, grinder, electrical system, casing) followed by micro disassembly using screwdrivers, utility knives, and a crowbar — revealed 33 individual components.

Material identification used magnet tests, water buoyancy tests, density estimation (water displacement), and combustion tests for unlabeled plastics. Manufacturing identification relied on geometric evidence: ejector pin marks, parting lines, draft angles for injection molding; shear bands and burr orientation for stamping.

Core BOM · key components

ComponentMaterialMassProcess

Main casingABS124gInjection molding
Motor coreLow carbon steel154gStamping
Vibration damperButyl rubber (IIR)10gInjection molding
Grinding chamberMartensitic stainless steel26gDeep drawing
BladesMartensitic stainless steel4gStamping
Fasteners (×10)Stainless steel10gCold heading + thread rolling

Macro disassembly · four functional subcomponents: (1) Lid · (2) Grinder · (3) Electrical components · (4) Casing & Brush

Complete teardown board · all 33 components identified · Red circle: motor & accessories · Blue: grinding chamber, blades, damper · Yellow: socket components · Green: casing & connectors

03 · Manufacturing Analysis

Every mark
tells a story.

Each manufacturing process leaves physical evidence on the part. By reading these marks systematically, four distinct processes were identified — each chosen for a specific engineering reason.

Injection Molding · ABS casing + PMMA lid

Identified via ejector pin marks, complex internal ribbing, and subtle parting lines. Core advantage: part consolidation — support posts, locating holes, and press-fit edges are all integrated into a single shot, eliminating additional fasteners.

Deep Drawing · grinding chamber

The stainless-steel grinding chamber is formed via deep drawing, producing a completely seamless internal structure — critical for preventing coffee grounds residue and bacterial growth. Strain hardening during forming allows 0.5mm wall thickness with high structural rigidity.

Stamping · motor laminations + blades

Identified by distinct shear zones and break zones on metal edges, and fine burrs on one side from mold gaps. Multi-station progressive stamping completes mounting holes and edge flanges rapidly, ensuring tight fit tolerances between motor and casing.

Cold Heading + Thread Rolling · screws

Confirmed by swarf-free surface and thread major diameter slightly larger than shank. Thread rolling compresses rather than cuts — 100% material utilisation, no chips, fibres intact. Fastest and most efficient route for standardised fasteners.

Why these materials?

ABS casing

Impact resistance 200–400 J/m absorbs motor vibration; processing fluidity optimises injection cycle time.

PMMA lid

92% light transmittance (surpasses most glass); higher surface hardness than PC resists coffee-bean scratching; excellent UV stability.

Martensitic stainless steel blades

Yield strength >500 MPa after quench/temper; HRC 50+ hardness; chromium oxide film resists coffee-oil corrosion. Centrifugal stress at 20,000 RPM ≈ 10.8 MPa — safety factor >46×.

Butyl rubber (IIR) damper

High loss tangent (tan δ) filters motor harmonics, preventing cyclic stress fatigue in the grinding chamber.

04 · Eco Audit · ANSYS Granta EduPack

The material
phase dominates.

Quantitative Eco-Audit using CES EduPack Level 1 database reveals a stark polarisation in lifecycle impact. The material acquisition phase accounts for 63.4% of total embodied energy and 66.4% of the carbon footprint. This finding defines the entire redesign strategy.

Lifecycle embodied energy distribution · total 90.9 MJ

Lifecycle CO₂ footprint distribution · total 5.41 kg

Embodied energy by component · material phase breakdown (%)

CO₂ by component · manufacturing phase breakdown (%)

Hotspot 1 · Material

Material phase: 63.4% energy, 66.4% CO₂. Virgin ABS and PMMA are the primary polymer culprits — high embodied energy per kg from petrochemical processing.

Hotspot 2 · Motor

Copper coils and low-carbon steel laminations dominate component-level emissions due to intensive smelting and processing energy. Motor architecture untouched by redesign.

Hotspot 3 · End-of-Life

Non-removable press-fit connections and friction-welded casings prevent disassembly. DfD implementation could recover 31.7 MJ and −1.73 kg CO₂ at end-of-life.

05 · The Redesign Solution

From disposable
to sustainable
premium.

The redesign targets the material phase directly. Without changing the motor architecture (preserving the existing high-performance core), three interventions are applied to the casing system:

Material · rPET replacing virgin ABS

100% post-consumer recycled polyester (rPET) substitutes virgin ABS in all casing components. rPET has significantly lower embodied energy and carbon footprint per kg, and benefits from a mature industrial closed-loop recycling system with lower post-recycling performance degradation than ABS.

Manufacturing · thin-wall + honeycomb ribbing

Upgraded to thin-wall injection molding with honeycomb internal ribbing, reducing wall thickness 15–20% while maintaining structural rigidity. Validated by Ashby Plot performance index M = E^(1/3)/ρ·H_c. Shorter cooling times also reduce injection cycle time and transport weight.

Assembly · Design for Disassembly (DfD)

Modular architecture separates casing, motor, and electrical system into independently replaceable modules. Standardised screws and snap-fits replace welding and adhesive bonding — enabling maintenance, component replacement, and end-of-life material separation.

Surface · matte texture via sandblasting

Mold surface sandblasting creates a fine matte texture — shifting user perception on semantic scales from "cheap / synthetic / fingerprint-prone" toward "premium / earthy / durable". Matte also increases grip during operation vibrations.

Eco audit comparison · original vs redesign

Metric Original Redesign
Casing materialVirgin ABS100% rPET
Embodied energy90.9 MJ73.2 MJ −19.5%
CO₂ footprint5.41 kg4.53 kg −16.3%
EoL CO₂ recovery−1.73 kg−2.06 kg +19.1%
